summary: I grew up in rural Alabama, about fifty miles south of Montgomery, in a very small place known as Four Corners. Alabama was where I first witnessed segregation and racial discrimination firsthand, attending a one-room church school with secondhand books. My early experiences in Alabama shaped my desire to oppose the system of racial injustice that was deeply entrenched there.

Montgomery, Alabama, was only about fifty miles from where I grew up, and it played a pivotal role in the civil rights movement. In 1955, when I was fifteen, I first heard about the Montgomery bus boycott, which inspired me deeply. I listened to Martin Luther King Jr.'s sermons broadcast from Montgomery and followed the boycott through my grandfather’s subscription to the Montgomery Advertiser.

The place I grew up is called Four Corners, Alabama, a tiny spot so small it’s hard to find on a map. Martin Luther King Jr. himself referred to it by that name. It’s near Troy, Alabama, and it was there on my family’s land that I experienced the harsh realities of segregation and poverty during my childhood.

Troy, Alabama, is a small town close to where I grew up in Four Corners. It had a population of about seven thousand and was a place where the signs of segregation were very visible, like 'colored only' and 'white only' signs in public places. Growing up near Troy, I saw firsthand the daily realities of racial discrimination.

I first heard of Martin Luther King Jr. when I was fifteen in 1955, through his sermons broadcast on a local Montgomery radio station. His eloquent preaching, especially his message to the church at Corinth, inspired me deeply because he focused on the struggles of black people in Montgomery rather than distant heavenly promises. His leadership during the Montgomery bus boycott became a beacon of hope for me and many others fighting segregation.

The Montgomery bus boycott of 1955 was a defining moment for me as a young person growing up near Montgomery. I followed the boycott through radio, television, and my grandfather’s Montgomery Advertiser newspaper. Seeing fifty thousand black people walk the streets for over a year rather than ride segregated buses gave me hope and inspired my commitment to civil rights.

Martin Luther King Jr. preached a sermon based on Paul the Apostle’s message to the church at Corinth, which I heard on the radio when I was fifteen. King adapted Paul’s letter to speak directly to the struggles of black Christians in America, especially those facing segregation in Montgomery. This sermon was particularly powerful and helped shape my understanding of faith and social justice.

My grandfather had a subscription to the Montgomery Advertiser, which was crucial for our family to keep up with the civil rights events happening nearby. Although we couldn’t afford our own subscription, we read the paper after he was done. It was through the Advertiser that I followed the developments of the Montgomery bus boycott and other local news.

I went to college in Nashville, Tennessee, starting in 1957, which was many miles away from my rural Alabama home. Nashville was known as a citadel of education in the South, with many colleges and churches that were more progressive. It was there that I felt freer to get involved in civil rights activities, including joining the youth chapter of the NAACP and participating in the Nashville sit-ins.

entity: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
summary: While studying in Nashville, I became a member of the youth chapter of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP). I even tried to organize a campus chapter at the American Baptist Theological Seminary, though that effort was blocked by the school administration. Nonetheless, my involvement with the NAACP was an important part of my early civil rights activism.

I was a student at the American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville, where I attempted to organize a NAACP campus chapter. Unfortunately, the seminary was supported by the Southern Baptist Convention and the National Baptist Convention, which led the president to deny our organizing efforts. Despite this, I remained active in civil rights work through other local organizations.

The Southern Baptist Convention was a significant influence on the American Baptist Theological Seminary where I studied. During the late 1950s, the Convention was largely white and conservative, which contributed to the seminary president’s decision to block the formation of a NAACP chapter on campus. This reflected the broader resistance to civil rights activism within some religious institutions at the time.

The National Baptist Convention, USA, Inc. was another religious organization supporting the American Baptist Theological Seminary during my time there. Like the Southern Baptist Convention, it was predominantly white and conservative in that era, which influenced the seminary’s stance against allowing a NAACP chapter. This environment made organizing civil rights activities on campus challenging.

The Nashville sit-ins began in late 1959 and early 1960 as a direct action against segregated lunch counters. We were well-prepared through weekly nonviolent workshops led by Reverend Kelly Miller Smith and the Nashville Christian Leadership Council. On the first day, over five hundred students from various backgrounds peacefully occupied seats at Woolworth, Kresge’s, and McClellan’s, refusing to leave despite being denied service, marking a key moment in the civil rights movement.

Reverend Kelly Miller Smith was the pastor of a local black Baptist church in Nashville and the president of the Nashville Christian Leadership Council. He played a crucial role in preparing us for the sit-ins by leading weekly nonviolent direct action workshops in 1959. His leadership helped us understand Gandhi’s philosophy and organize effective, peaceful protests against segregation.

The Nashville Christian Leadership Council, led locally by Reverend Kelly Miller Smith, was instrumental in organizing and supporting the sit-ins in 1959 and 1960. It was an arm of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and provided the framework for our nonviolent workshops and direct action strategies. Their guidance was essential in preparing students for peaceful protests downtown.

The Southern Christian Leadership Conference was the parent organization of the Nashville Christian Leadership Council, which was active in civil rights work in Nashville. Through this connection, we received support and guidance in organizing nonviolent protests like the Nashville sit-ins. The SCLC’s philosophy, inspired by leaders like Martin Luther King Jr., shaped much of our approach to direct action.

Mahatma Gandhi’s teachings on nonviolence and civil disobedience were central to our preparation for the Nashville sit-ins. During the weekly workshops in 1959, we studied his philosophy and the history of his struggle in India and South Africa. Gandhi’s example inspired us to adopt peaceful resistance as a powerful tool against segregation and injustice.

India was the country where Mahatma Gandhi led a nonviolent struggle for independence, which we studied extensively during our workshops in Nashville. His success in India provided a model for civil rights activism in the United States. Learning about India’s fight against colonialism helped us understand the power of peaceful resistance.

South Africa was another country where Gandhi worked to resolve issues of racial injustice through nonviolent means. We studied his efforts there during our workshops in Nashville, which helped us connect global struggles for justice with our own fight against segregation. South Africa’s example reinforced the importance of peaceful direct action.

The Greensboro sit-ins, which began on February 1, 1960, were a catalyst for our own sit-in movement in Nashville. After hearing from Douglas Moore, a young minister, we were motivated to organize mass sit-ins in support of the students in North Carolina. The Greensboro sit-ins showed us the effectiveness of direct, peaceful protest against segregation.

Fisk University in Nashville was a central gathering place for students involved in the sit-ins. On the first day of the protests, over five hundred students assembled in front of the Fisk chapel before heading downtown. Fisk’s role as a historically black university made it a natural hub for organizing civil rights activities.

Woolworth’s lunch counter in downtown Nashville was one of the primary sites of our sit-in protests in early 1960. We occupied every seat in the restaurant, dressed neatly and studying while being denied service. The sit-ins at Woolworth’s became a symbol of peaceful resistance against segregation in the South.

Kresge’s was another downtown Nashville store where we held sit-ins during the civil rights protests. Along with Woolworth and McClellan’s, Kresge’s lunch counters were segregated, and we challenged that by peacefully occupying seats. These sit-ins were part of a larger coordinated effort to desegregate public facilities in Nashville.

McClellan’s was one of the stores in downtown Nashville where we staged sit-ins as part of the civil rights movement. We would sit quietly at the lunch counters, dressed as if for church, and study while being refused service. McClellan’s, along with Woolworth and Kresge’s, became a focal point for challenging segregation in the city.

While the transcript does not explicitly mention 'We Shall Overcome,' it was a key anthem during the civil rights movement that inspired many of us involved in the Nashville sit-ins and other protests. The song embodied the spirit of nonviolent resistance and hope that guided our actions. It was often sung during workshops and demonstrations to strengthen our resolve.

Douglas Moore was a young minister who reached out to us in Nashville after the Greensboro sit-ins began in 1960. He asked what the students in Nashville could do to support the North Carolina students, which pushed us to organize our own mass sit-ins. His encouragement helped spark the coordinated direct action that became the Nashville sit-in movement.
